infons.title: Keywords Subject terms

passages.text: Ensuring the integrity of AI models: a blockchain-based approach for protecting medical imaging training data

The development of trustworthy AI models is crucial, particularly for critical medical applications such as brain tumor detection using MRI images. However, medical images are being increasingly targeted by adversarial attacks to compromise the diagnostic accuracy of AI-based solutions. Adversarial attacks lead to deliberate perturbations in the medical imaging dataset, which will deceive the functioning of AI models. While these perturbations are visually imperceptible to human beings, they can cause AI models to malfunction if trained on adversarial images. To enhance the trust of healthcare professionals and patients in AI-based diagnosis of brain tumors, this research article presents a novel blockchain-based framework that utilizes Hyperledger Fabric and Private IPFS. This framework will safeguard MRI scans for brain tumor detection from unauthorized access & tampering by adversaries by decentralizing data storage and access control while ensuring data provenance. Hyperledger Fabric and private IPFS enable secure and tamper-proof dataset storage and sharing, leading to reliable and adversarially robust AI-based solutions. Experimental evaluations of the proposed framework demonstrate decentralized cryptographic assurance of image integrity in a permissioned blockchain network of the healthcare and AI fraternity. Performance of this defense strategy is validated using Hyperledger Caliper.
Introduction

Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a disruptive invention in the discipline of medical imaging, notably in the early detection and diagnosis of critical diseases like brain tumor. AI-based diagnosis, particularly deep learning models, can learn subtle patterns and anomalies from high-dimensional imaging data like magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data. If AI- models are trained on extensive and precise datasets, it has demonstrated the highest diagnostic accuracy, supported faster decision-making and decreasing the workload on healthcare professionals. An AI-based system’s success solely depends on the quality and integrity of the dataset used to train the AI model. In the case of MRI data, it has to be unbiased and tamperproof. A model’s performance can be significantly degraded by the inclusion of manipulated or corrupted data, either accidentally or deliberately, which is precipitated by technical artifacts, human error, or malicious intent.
Adversarial attack surface of AI-based brain tumor detection.

Figure 1 illustrates the adversarial threat landscape across both the training and testing phases of AI-based brain tumor classification. During the training phase, an adversary may perform data poisoning attacks by injecting adversarially manipulated MRI images into the training dataset. These perturbations are typically imperceptible to human observers but can significantly bias the learned model parameters, resulting in a classifier that is systematically compromised. Once trained on such corrupted data, the model may exhibit high confidence yet incorrect predictions. In the testing (inference) phase, adversarial attacks target the deployed model by subtly modifying benign test images to induce misclassification, such as predicting a benign tumor as malignant or vice versa. The diagram highlights that both attack vectors exploit the absence of integrity guarantees on the medical imaging data pipeline, either before or after model deployment.

This threat model assumes that adversaries may possess access to data storage or transmission channels but do not control the underlying cryptographic primitives or trusted system components. Consequently, protecting the integrity, provenance, and authorized access of medical images prior to model training and inference is critical.. In medical imaging, such failures can result in a malignant tumor being incorrectly classified as benign or the tumor being missed altogether, which can have life-threatening consequences. It enforces the importance of developing powerful countermeasures against this threat to avoid compromised AI models being deployed in healthcare.

Although a lot of research has focused on enhancing the robustness of AI models against adversarial examples on the algorithmic side, there is still a large gap in ensuring that the datasets they are trained on are secure and traceable. To solve this problem, it is important to protect imaging data at the source, i.e., the moment of creation at the diagnostic centers, prior to being included in a dataset or being utilized in any downstream application. The medical images are taken with various scanning modalities and transferred, stored, and shared in many systems, which makes them susceptible to tampering at every point. Therefore, the integrity and provenance of these images back to the source are critical to the maintenance of trust in AI-based diagnostic systems. In this research, the authors have suggested a new approach to the security of medical imaging data based on the integration of Hyperledger Fabric, a permissioned blockchain system, and Private IPFS (InterPlanetary File System), a distributed file storage system. This framework directly addresses these vulnerabilities by preventing adversarially manipulated images from entering the AI pipeline and by enabling verifiable audit trails for all data access and usage events.
Related work

The growing use of deep learning (DL) models in high-stakes areas, especially medical diagnosis, has prompted both empirical and theoretical studies on how to protect against adversarial manipulation of the training data. The risk of adversarial attacks, which cause invisible perturbations that deceive AI systems, has stimulated the invention of techniques aimed at increasing the integrity of datasets and robustness of models. This discussion reviews the main methods that are in existence, evaluates their applicability in medical imaging, and highlights their different limitations.
Adversarial training and input sanitization
It is computationally expensive, requiring significant overhead to generate and incorporate adversarial samples.
Models trained this way may become overfit to specific types of attacks and remain vulnerable to unseen ones.
The approach does not address the issue of dataset integrity; it assumes the training data is already available and legitimate.

Adversarial training is one of the most widely studied techniques to improve model resilience. This approach involves generating adversarial examples during training and including them in the training set to help the model learn more robust representations. While adversarial training can increase a model’s ability to resist known attack types (e.g., FGSM, PGD), it suffers from several key drawbacks:

Input sanitization methods, such as JPEG compression, noise filtering, or autoencoders, attempt to “clean” adversarial inputs before feeding them to the model. However, these are often brittle and inconsistent, as advanced attacks can bypass sanitization with minimal modifications. Furthermore, these methods do not ensure traceability or authenticity of the data, making them unsuitable for regulated fields like healthcare.
Dataset watermarking and fingerprinting

Another line of defense includes digital watermarking and fingerprinting, where a unique signature is embedded into the data or model to detect unauthorized use or tampering. While watermarking can be useful in intellectual property protection, its application to medical datasets is limited. First, watermarking often requires altering the original image, which may not be permissible or reliable in clinical settings. Second, it does not prevent adversarial attacks; it merely detects them after the fact and does not maintain an immutable audit trail of dataset usage.
Trusted execution environments (TEE) and secure hardware
Have limited memory, unsuitable for training large-scale models.
Require specialized hardware, which may not be available in all diagnostic or research environments.
Still depends on the assumption that the input dataset has not already been manipulated prior to training.

Recent work has explored the use of secure hardware environments such as Intel SGX to ensure that AI training occurs on untampered data. Although TEEs offer confidentiality and integrity during computation, they:
Blockchain and decentralized storage

A more recent direction has focused on blockchain-based data provenance systems. Blockchain offers tamper-resistant, auditable records that can be used to track data ownership and modifications. Some systems combine this with IPFS, a decentralized file system, to ensure both availability and integrity. However, most existing solutions are not tailored to the healthcare domain, and many do not address the specific needs of private data (such as MRI images governed by HIPAA or GDPR).

Moreover, the majority of these frameworks operate on public blockchains, which raise concerns regarding data confidentiality and latency. The lack of fine-grained access control and organizational boundaries further limits their applicability in clinical or research workflows involving multiple stakeholders (e.g., diagnostic centers, data custodians, and researchers).
Limitations and research gap
Summary of existing techniques to combat adversarial attacks.

infons.xml:

In summary, current solutions are either focused solely on improving model robustness or offer post hoc detection and response to data corruption. None of them provide end-to-end dataset protection from the point of origin (e.g., diagnostic centers) with traceable access, decentralized verification, and privacy guarantees as summarized in Table 1.
We propose a permissioned, healthcare-specific blockchain framework using Hyperledger Fabric integrated with Private IPFS, explicitly designed for sensitive MRI datasets.
Unlike public blockchain–IPFS solutions, our framework enforces organizational identity, role-based access control, and private data collections, ensuring regulatory compliance.
We introduce a dataset-level adversarial defense mechanism that prevents data poisoning by guaranteeing cryptographic image integrity prior to AI model training.
The framework is fully implemented and validated using Hyperledger Caliper, demonstrating feasibility under concurrent workloads.
Contributions of this work are summarized as follows:
Proposed framework

The proposed framework focuses on dataset-level adversarial threats, which pose significant risks prior to AI model training. Specifically, we have considered data poisoning attack, where malicious or manipulated images are added into the dataset. Image tampering involves subtle or deliberate modification of stored MRI scans and unauthorized data injection by malicious or compromised entities. The focus of these attacks is to corrupt the integrity of the training dataset while remaining undetected by conventional access control mechanisms.

This research addresses that current research gap by introducing a Hyperledger Fabric-based permissioned blockchain integrated with Private IPFS, enabling secure image uploads, immutable logging, and authenticated access creating a tamper-evident environment from image generation to model training. This approach uniquely combines trust, auditability, and privacy, making it well-suited for adversarial defense in medical AI systems.

The experimental evaluation uses publicly available MRI brain tumor images obtained from the Brain Tumor Detection (Br35H) dataset. The dataset consists of T1-weight magnetic resonance images representing both tumor and non-tumor cases. All images are utilized to demonstrate the secure storage, registration, and controlled access mechanisms of the proposed framework. Since the primary objective of this study is to evaluate dataset integrity and provenance rather than classification performance, the dataset is employed without extensive preprocessing beyond format normalization required for storage and retrieval.
System Architecture for protecting medical imaging data from adversarial attacks.
Workflow diagram for proposed system.

Our proposed system architecture involves three core entities as illustrated in Fig. 2: the DataOwner (e.g., diagnostic center), the DataCustodian (a trusted party responsible for uploading and managing data), and the ResearchCenter (an AI developer or medical research institution). The DataCustodian uploads MRI images to a private IPFS network, and the corresponding content hashes are permanently stored on the Hyperledger Fabric ledger via a custom MRI Image Chaincode. Access logs are also logged by the system, and access is limited to authorized entities (e.g., the ResearchCenter) who should be able to request and retrieve the original IPFS hash of an image. The access requests and approvals are all safely logged on the blockchain and hence offer complete auditability. This guarantees that future access or tampering is identifiable and can be tracked back with complete auditability. Thus, it eliminates the risk of adversarial tampering by decentralizing storage of medical imaging data and verification, and it allows trusted sharing of data with authorized entities. The framework establishes the basis of creating AI models robust to attackers and models that are built to protect patient privacy, hence giving healthcare professionals and patients a higher level of trust in the system. The following Fig. 3 represents the detailed architecture and workflow of the implemented framework.
The workflow is divided into following steps:
Step 1: peer registration (Fabric Setup)

infons.xml:
All organizations must register and enroll their peers into the Fabric Membership Service Provider (MSP)

1. Identity Registration

Each org (DataOwner, DataCustodian, ResearchCenter, Orderer) requests peer identity from Fabric CA

CA generates enrollment certificate (ECert) and TLS certificate

2. MSP Setup

Each organization creates its own MSP directory with:

cacerts/ → CA certs

signcerts/ → peer certificates

keystore/ → private keys

3. Genesis Block & Channel Creation

Orderer generates genesis block for the system channel

Application channel (e.g., BTDchannel) is created

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$${Peer}_{\mathrm{i}} \in {Channel}_{BTD}$$\end{document}

4. Anchor Peer Definition

Each org defines at least one anchor peer to allow inter-org communication

5. Peer Registration Result

After registration, the Fabric network is a decentralized permissioned graph:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mathcal{N}=\{DataOwner.peer, DataCustodian.peer\mathrm{ResearchCenter}.\mathrm{peer}, Orderer.peer\}$$\end{document}

The proposed framework is applied on a permissioned Hyperledger Fabric network to ensure that it executes deterministically, manages identities, and finalizes transaction latency, which is appropriate for healthcare settings. Each of the participating entities are verified by a Certificate Authority (CA) which defines the limits of trust among organizations that are necessary for controlled medical data handling.
Step 2: private IPFS setup

infons.xml:
A private IPFS network is configured with swarm keys for authentication

Each node (DataCustodian, ResearchCenter, DataOwner) runs a Kubo node

Nodes join the same private swarm:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$\mathrm{IPF}{S}_{net}=\{\mathrm{Nod}{e}_{DO},\hspace{0.17em}N{\mathrm{od}e}_{DC},\hspace{0.17em}N{\mathrm{od}e}_{RC}\}$$\end{document}

This ensures data is only shared among authorized nodes

passages.text: A private IPFS network is configured with swarm keys for authenticationEach node (DataCustodian, ResearchCenter, DataOwner) runs a Kubo nodeNodes join the same private swarm:This ensures data is only shared among authorized nodes

Since medical imaging data is large, a direct storage of MRI images on-chain would have a tremendous effect on scalability and performance. Thus, the suggested framework will implement an off-chain storage model, in which case the raw MRI images will be stored in a Private IPFS network, and only the cryptographic references will be stored on a blockchain. Shared swarm keys are used to configure a private IPFS swarm to guarantee that only authenticated healthcare stakeholders would be able to engage in the data exchange.
Step 3: image generation

The framework explicitly ensures that medical images are secured at the generation stage, before any further AI-based training or data communications. This design addresses adversarial threats at the dataset level, by making sure that protection of integrity starts at the diagnostic center itself. DataOwner generates a medical image I (e.g., MRI scan).
Step 4: storage in private IPFS

infons.xml:
Image I is uploaded into IPFS private network

IPFS returns a Content Identifier (CID):

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$CID=IPFS\left(I\right)$$\end{document}

CID uniquely identifies the stored image

passages.text: Image I is uploaded into IPFS private network IPFS returns a Content Identifier (CID):CID uniquely identifies the stored image

When uploaded into the private IPFS network, each MRI image is given a Content Identifier (CID), a cryptographic hash of the content of the images. Any alteration of the image and even the tiniest adversarial perturbations cause a change in CID, allowing to be able to detect the tampering instantly. This measure improves content addressable integrity verification and it is independent of storing large files on-chain.
Step 5: blockchain registration

infons.xml:
DataCustodian registers image reference on Fabric:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$T=\{\hspace{0.17em}CID,\hspace{0.25em}H\left(I\right),\hspace{0.25em}I{D}_{DO},\hspace{0.25em}Si{g}_{DO}\left(CID\parallel H\left(I\right)\right)\hspace{0.17em}\}$$\end{document}

Transaction is signed by DataOwner’s private key and committed via RAFT consensus

passages.text: DataCustodian registers image reference on Fabric:Transaction is signed by DataOwner’s private key and committed via RAFT consensus

The resulting CID obtained by IPFS is stored on a blockchain with the help of a custom smart contract (chaincode) to facilitate traceability and access control. The chaincode consists of the application logic of the image registration, access control and audit logging. The UploadImage() function registers the image identifier, CID, uploader identity and the timestamp on the ledger. Membership Service Provider (MSP) identifiers enforce role-based access control by making sure that registration operations are only done by authorized organizations.
Step 6: researchcenter access request

All the access to the medical images is not made directly to the files but is channeled through the authorization based on blockchain in order to avoid unauthorized use of data. This design allows sharing of data in a controlled manner and retains the privacy of patients and auditability.

infons.xml:
ResearchCenter requests image access:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$Req=\{\hspace{0.17em}I{D}_{RC},\hspace{0.25em}CID,\hspace{0.25em}N\hspace{0.17em}\}$$\end{document}

where N = nonce

Request digitally signed:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$Si{g}_{RC}={S}_{S{K}_{RC}}\left(Req\right)$$\end{document}

passages.text: ResearchCenter requests image access:where N = nonceRequest digitally signed:
The ResearchCenter presents an on-chain access request with the RequestAccess() function and this is digitally signed and has a time stamp to prevent replay attacks or impersonation attacks.

infons.xml:
Peers validate ResearchCenter’s request:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$V\left(Si{g}_{RC},\hspace{0.17em}P{K}_{RC}\right)\to \{True,False\}$$\end{document}

If valid, DataCustodian approves access and records it on-chain

passages.text: Peers validate ResearchCenter’s request:If valid, DataCustodian approves access and records it on-chain
Step 7: consensus & access approval
Step 8: retrieval from private IPFS

After the access is provided, the image is downloaded by the ResearchCenter through the direct IPFS network. Before it is used, a verification of the image integrity is done by computing its hash once more and comparing it with the CID stored therein on the blockchain.

infons.xml:
ResearchCenter retrieves the raw image from IPFS:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$I=IPFS\_get\left(CID\right)$$\end{document}

Integrity verified by recomputing hash:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$H(I)={H}_{ledger}$$\end{document}

passages.text: ResearchCenter retrieves the raw image from IPFS:Integrity verified by recomputing hash:
Any mismatch will trigger an alarm to tampering by not allowing adversarially altered images to pass through the AI learning process.
Step *: consensus–commit to ledger

infons.xml:
Orderer collects transactions and batches them into a block B

RAFT consensus ensures agreement:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$Commit\left(B\right)\Leftrightarrow \forall Pee{r}_{i}\in Channel,\hspace{0.25em}V\left(B,P{K}_{orderer}\right)=True$$\end{document}

Block B is appended to the ledger across all peers:

\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath} \usepackage{wasysym} \usepackage{amsfonts} \usepackage{amssymb} \usepackage{amsbsy} \usepackage{mathrsfs} \usepackage{upgreek} \setlength{\oddsidemargin}{-69pt} \begin{document}$$Ledger=Ledger\cup B$$\end{document}

passages.text: Orderer collects transactions and batches them into a block BRAFT consensus ensures agreement:Block B is appended to the ledger across all peers:

The blockchain layer uses a RAFT-based ordering service. RAFT is chosen due to its conclusive finality and fault tolerance of crashes in permissioned networks. In contrast to probabilistic consensus mechanisms, such as those of public blockchains, RAFT ensures that after a transaction is committed, a transaction is final and globally consistent between peers. This design also reduces the delays in transaction confirmations and removes any forks, which are essential in time sensitive medical processes and auditability.
Experimental setup
System configuration.

To evaluate the efficacy and performance of our proposed architecture for managing secure MRI datasets, we developed a comprehensive experimental framework comprising a permissioned blockchain network built on Hyperledger Fabric, a customized chaincode (smart contract) for the administration of image metadata and access logs, Private Data Collections (PDC) to regulate data visibility among organizations, a Private IPFS node for secure image storage, and Hyperledger Caliper for performance benchmarking. Docker and Docker Compose are employed exclusively for containerized deployment and orchestration of the experimental environment (Table 2).
Hyperledger fabric network configuration
Data Owner, such as Diagnostic Center, is in responsible for generating and uploading MRI images.
Data Custodian regulates access rights, IPFS storage, and image registration.
The Research Center has the authority to request and obtain images for the purpose of training models.
There are three organizations which are part of our blockchain network, each of which represents a distinct function in the life cycle of MRI data:
Directory structure of organizations and hyperledger fabric network up and running.
Every organization has a certificate authority (CA), a peer node, an administrator, and a user identity as demonstrated in Fig. 4.

We implemented the network on Fabric v2.5 running RAFT consensus, and defined only one channel (btdchannel) to be used in all transactions between three organizations. Docker Compose was used to containerize and orchestrate the Fabric network.
Chaincode for image management
UploadImage(imageID, ipfsHash, uploaderOrg) is the function that register new image with IPFS hash and related metadata.
ResearchCenter can request access by using the RequestAccess(imageID) function, which logs the request along with its identity and timestamp.
If access has been granted, then GetImage(imageID) function retrieves metadata.
GetAccessLog(imageID) function enables auditing of every access request made for a particular image.
Invoking Chaincode Lifecycle.
To manage image records, a unique Go chaincode was developed and deployed on the channel as demonstrated in Fig. 5. The primary functions are as follows:

With role-based access enforced by MSPID checks, this chaincode ensures that only authorized organizations can perform specific operations. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is included in the chaincode using GetMSPID() to guarantee that, only DataCustodianMSP is able to upload images or store private information. Only ResearchCenterMSP has the ability to read or request access to confidential metadata. For improved traceability, it further includes the requester’s MSP ID to access logs.
Private data collections (PDC)
Private data collection and code snippet of collection.json for private data collection.

Critical image metadata (such as Patient personal information and access logs) have been saved through Private Data Collections between DataCustodian and DataOwner in order to improve data confidentiality. Only image hashes and permissioned data remained accessible to the ResearchCenter through chaincode queries. Collection policies were defined in collections.json with the following configuration. As shown in following Fig. 6 privateImageMetadata will be exclusively shared between the Research Center and Data Custodian.

This mechanism ensured that while image access was traceable, private organizational data remained confidential. Rather than creating a new channel we added private data collection. As adding new channel cause overhead on resources.
Private IPFS node integration
Private IPFS initialization.

The integration of a private IPFS node using Kubo v0.30.0 within a Dockerized environment demonstrates a secure and decentralized approach to content distribution. By initializing the node with a shared swarm.key, the deployment ensures that only authenticated peers can participate in the network, effectively isolating it from the public IPFS ecosystem. The deliberate removal of default bootstrap peers further reinforces this isolation, preventing unintended external connections and enhancing data privacy. Exposing the local API and Gateway interfaces allows for seamless interaction with the node, enabling efficient uploading and retrieval of image assets via HTTP and CLI commands. Connectivity verification through ipfs swarm peers and ipfs id confirms successful peer discovery and network integrity, validating the configuration as demonstrated in Fig. 7.

The proposed framework represents a systematic integration of private IPFS which is decentralized storage along with blockchain enabled data provenance for critical medical data in the form of images. Every image is authenticated by its CID returned by IPFS. Chaincode enables us to store Images immutably and facilitating secure, traceable and privacy preserved medical image management wherein it protects images from potential adversarial attacks. Every time an image is registered, requested to be accessed, approved or retrieved all this is invariably recorded on the blockchain. This allows full auditing, regulatory and post incident forensic analysis that traditional centralized storage alone or federated learning alone cannot provide.
System performance

Hyperledger Caliper (v0.5.0) was integrated to evaluate the resilience and efficiency of the implemented blockchain network with the Hyperledger Fabric setup to conduct performance benchmarking under varying transaction loads. The configuration utilised the Fabric Gateway SDK for Node.js as the system under test (SUT) adapter with specific parameters including the contract ID (dataset-Contract), identity credentials (User1 from DataCustodianMSP), and workload modules (uploadImage.js and requestAccess.js). Simulated transaction rounds ranged from 40 to 200 concurrent image uploads and access requests, emulating real-world scenarios involving multiple diagnostic centers and research institutions.
Performance of implemented System using Hyperledger Caliper.

The collected key performance indicators as mentioned in Fig. 8 included transaction throughput (TPS), latency, success/failure ratio, and resource utilization. TPS measured the number of valid transactions executed per second, providing insight into the system’s ability to process image records immediately without bottlenecks. Latency metrics—average, maximum, and minimum—record the processing time of a transaction from start to finish, from transmission to final commit in the registry. For image registration, the latency extended from the DataCustodian to the Orderer and peer consensus, while for access requests, it followed the ResearchCenter request until validation and commit. The low latency profile indicated the rapid availability of MRI data for research purposes. The success/failure ratio confirmed the robustness of the blockchain-IPFS integration, with successful transactions involving CID storage, hash checking, and registry commits, while failures are considered from signature mismatches, recovery errors, or peer failures which is 0 for our proposed framework.
Security assessment

Particularly in sensitive disciplines like brain tumor detection, it is imperative to guarantee the accuracy and dependability of datasets used in artificial intelligence (AI) models. The proposed blockchain integrated architecture applies a multi-layered protection mechanism to protect MRI datasets against various malicious attacks.

The significant feature of this Hyperledger Fabric with a private IPFS network is to provide the off-chain storage of image, and ensure the safe and controlled metadata management. Each uploaded image results in the creation of a hash (CID (Container ID)) by IPFS. This CID has been permanently attached to Hyperledger Fabric Blockchain Network to bar the option of manipulating this dataset. Since any small change in the image will cause the change in hash thus will cause the mismatch of hash which means the violation of integrity. Hyperledger fabric membership service provider (MSP) limits access to blockchain network by unauthorized persons. The information of the patient was then gathered privately with the help of the feature known as the Private Data Collection (PDC) which allows the information to be accessed only by the authorized organizations.

All access requests to images on chain are recorded in this framework and they are useful in restraining adversaries and auditing all changes. The consensus algorithm helps with timestamp, multi-peer approval and unique transaction signatures to avoid replay and injection attacks. TLS encryption and signature validation protects against man-in-the-middle attacks. Private IPFS makes it efficient to allow only trusted nodes to involve in data exchange using a shared swarm key. Integration of Hyperledger Fabric and Private IPFS deliver a strong, reliable, and access-controlled framework for sustainable AI-based medical research.
Limitations and future work

While this proposed framework demonstrates effective integrity protection and controlled sharing of medical imaging data, several limitations should be acknowledged. Interoperability with existing hospital information systems, such as Electronic Medical Record (EMR) systems and Picture Archiving and Communication Systems (PACS), remains a practical challenge. The heterogeneous integration with clinical infrastructures may need more middleware and standardized interfaces, which is not discussed in the present implementation. The hospitals systems are frequently based on various, vendor-specific architectures and clinical standards, so integrating with the blockchain-based frameworks is not a trivially easy task. Although the present research is aimed at the infrastructure layer of securing medical imaging data, the interoperability with the already existing clinical workflows is also an essential future research direction.

The permissioned blockchain design facilitates controlled access, auditability, and traceability of medical imaging data, but complete adherence to the healthcare regulations, including the HIPAA and GDPR, is not limited to technical protection only. Compliance with regulations would entail extensive regulatory frameworks, such as the explicit data ownership, data consent management, data retention policies, as well as the data subject rights mechanisms, such as access, correction, and erasure. Although the proposed framework provides privacy protection with the help of role-based access control, organizational identity management, and personal data collections, the implementation of regulatory requirements is finally defined by the institution-specific deployment policies, operation processes and legal interpretations, which differs across jurisdictions. The enforcement of regulations, in turn, should be imposed as the result of a conglomerate of the proposed technical architecture and organizational and legal measures taken by the involved healthcare institutions.

In blockchain-based infrastructures, Peer execution, consensus, and ledger maintenance overheads are generally introduced. Even though off-chain storage can dramatically decrease the cost of processing on-chain, its widespread use in various healthcare facilities might need an additional optimization to ensure that the trade-off between security assurance and resource consumption is balanced.

Moreover, the suggested framework is data-level security before AI model training, and it is not specifically plotted to the privacy-preserving distributed learning paradigm. Future research will be on the integration with federated learning frameworks, which allows managing data securely and auditability, and the decentralized training of models without sharing raw data. This integration would also boost the confidence of collaborative development of AI in medicine.
Conclusion

This work includes a well-built and secure infrastructure, using Hyperledger Fabric and private IPFS, to protect information on MRI brain tumor imaging. Since the collection of data is received at the diagnostic center, the proposed system will ensure the privacy of data, its authenticity, and traceability. It can only allow authorized users such as data custodians and research centers to use sensitive data under a controlled setup through role-based access control and private datasets. Chaincode logs transactions, has high access control and stores image hashes and information in a ledger. The inclusion of a decentralized, tamper-resistant storage layer to MRI images in the private IPFS system complicates the abilities of adversaries to modify or fabricate data, which is not a part of the limited range of authorized processes.

The Hyperledger Caliper is used to benchmark the UploadImage transactions and verify the performance of the system. The results indicate that the system is functional and there were 42 successful uploads and no failures. Transaction processing has a throughput of 1.8 transaction per second, which is the highest latency of 2.08 s, lowest latency of 0.07 s, an average latency of 1.49 s, and an upload rate of 2.0 transactions per second. These measurements show that the system can manage secure real-time image uploads without experiencing a additional overhead. We want to expand the architecture’s scalability in the future to accommodate extensive deployments across several hospitals and diagnostic facilities. In order to facilitate improved integration, future studies will also examine compatibility with electronic medical record systems and federated learning platforms.
